description: Metal–organic framework: Coordination networks and molecular sponges for gas storage 🧽
extended: Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) consist of metal ions or clusters coordinated to organic ligands to form one-, two-, or three-dimensional structures. They function as customizable molecular sponges, providing a "Lego block" framework with ultra-high surface areas that can be tuned for specific chemical guests. This architectural precision makes them ideal for high-capacity hydrogen storage, carbon capture, and targeted drug delivery.

description: Latent Space: ML for Material Science, active learning for chemical discovery 🔬
extended: Heather Kulik discusses how machine learning and AI are accelerating the discovery of new materials, specifically using active learning to navigate massive chemical search spaces. This approach is being applied to metal-organic frameworks for direct CO2 air capture, essentially using AI as a guide to snap together the right molecular Lego blocks for carbon sequestration.

description: The 2025 Nobel Prize in Chemistry: Metal Organic Frameworks, a lego block for precision chemicals
extended: Susumu Kitagawa, Richard Robson, and Omar Yaghi were awarded the Nobel Prize for the development of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), crystalline materials that use metal ions as cornerstones linked by organic molecules. "Lego blocks" for composing precise chemical structures, creating highly porous "molecular rooms" capable of harvesting water from desert air or capturing carbon dioxide.

description: Mirage: The Illusion of Visual Understanding
extended: "In the most extreme case, our model achieved the top rank on a standard chest X-ray question-answering benchmark without access to any images." —models act as “super guessers” acting on context clues, and hidden structures in the benchmarks.

description: China battery trio gain $70bn as Iran war sparks ‘paradigm shift’
extended: Not the way we hoped the climate transition would happen.

description: Suddenly, the US manufactures a ton of grid batteries | Canary Media
extended: Up from roughly zero capacity in 2024, US now on track to has capacity for 96 GWh of grid spec battery cells by end of 2026. Huge transformation, dependent on IRA as not cost competitive with China.

description: optimize_anything: A Universal API for Optimizing any Text Parameter - GEPA
extended: "The key insight is that a surprisingly wide range of problems can be formulated as optimizing a text artifact".

description: FFmpeg at Meta: Media Processing at Scale - Engineering at Meta
extended: "Meta executes ffmpeg and ffprobe binaries tens of billions of times a day" -- ffmpeg is one of the CLIs I've found that can still consistently break an AI's "brain".

description: AI Didn't Break the Senior Engineer Pipeline. It Showed That One Never Existed.
extended: “Most organizations have never had a model for developing software engineers. They had an environment that happened to produce growth, and they mistook the environment for a system.”

description: Integrated Capture and Conversion of Dilute CO2 Using an Oxygen Tolerant Porous Carbon Modified Gas Diffusion Electrode | ACS Energy Letters
extended: Lab-scale demonstration of 3rd wave direct air carbon caption addresses key challenges with DAC: it doesn't waste energy on oxygen, operates at room temperature without the superheating step to release CO2, and produces a valuable output (formic acid) vs something that needs to be sequestered.

description: prime-radiant-inc/clearance: A Markdown viewer for macOS
extended: A file browser for .md files from Jesse.
"I learned yesterday that an open problem I’d been working on for several weeks had just been solved by Claude Opus 4.6"

description: On the Return of the Floreana Giant Tortoise | Galápagos Conservancy
extended: “For the first time in 180 years, Floreana lineage giant tortoises set foot on Floreana Island once again.”

description: Paul Ford on “The A.I. Disruption Has Arrived, and It Sure Is Fun”
extended: ““All of the people I love hate this stuff, and all the people I hate love it. And yet, likely because of the same personality flaws that drew me to technology in the first place, I am annoyingly excited.” <= this.
